content.title: Types of Discontinuities
content.content:
Let $f$ be defined on an interval containing $c$ (except possibly at $c$ 
itself). We classify discontinuities at $c$:

1. **Removable discontinuity:** 
$\lim_{x \to c} f(x)$ exists but either $f(c)$ is undefined or 
$f(c) \neq \lim_{x \to c} f(x)$.

2. **Jump discontinuity:** Both one-sided 
limits exist but are unequal: $\lim_{x \to c^-} f(x) \neq \lim_{x \to c^+} f(x)$.

3. **Essential discontinuity:** 
At least one of the one-sided limits fails to exist (including the case 
of infinite limits or oscillation).

content.title: Types of Discontinuities
content.problem:
1. $f(x) = \frac{\sin x}{x}$ has a removable discontinuity at $x = 0$ 
(the limit is $1$).

2. The sign function $\text{sgn}(x) = \begin{cases} 1 & x > 0 \\ 0 & x = 0 \\ -1 & x < 0 \end{cases}$ 
has a jump discontinuity at $0$.

3. $f(x) = \sin(1/x)$ has an essential discontinuity at $0$ (oscillation).

content.title: Discontinuities of Monotone Functions
content.content:
If $f$ is monotone on $(a, b)$, then:

1. At each point $c \in (a, b)$, both one-sided limits exist.

2. The only discontinuities are jump discontinuities.

3. The set of discontinuities is at most countable.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
(a) Assume $f$ is increasing. For $c \in (a,b)$, the set $\{f(x) : x < c\}$ 
is bounded above by $f(c)$, so $\sup\{f(x) : x < c\}$ exists; this is 
$\lim_{x \to c^-} f(x)$.

(b) By (a), both one-sided limits exist, so discontinuities can only be jumps.

(c) If $f$ has a jump at $c$, the "jump interval" 
$(\lim_{x \to c^-} f(x), \lim_{x \to c^+} f(x))$ contains a rational number. 
Different jumps give disjoint intervals (by monotonicity), hence different 
rationals. Since $\mathbb{Q}$ is countable, so is the set of jumps.

content.title: The Dirichlet Function
content.problem:
The **Dirichlet function** is defined by
\[
D(x) = \begin{cases} 1 & x \in \mathbb{Q} \\ 0 & x \notin \mathbb{Q} \end{cases}
\]
This function is discontinuous at every point.
content.solution:
At any $c$, both rationals and irrationals are arbitrarily close to $c$ 
(by density). So any neighborhood of $c$ contains points where $D = 1$ 
and points where $D = 0$. Thus $\lim_{x \to c} D(x)$ does not exist.

content.title: Thomae's Function
content.problem:
**Thomae's function** is defined by
\[
T(x) = \begin{cases} 1/q & x = p/q \text{ in lowest terms}, q > 0 \\ 0 & x \notin \mathbb{Q} \end{cases}
\]
This function is continuous at every irrational and discontinuous at every 
rational.
